file_name: PlayboyEnterprisesInc_20090220_10-QA_EX-10.2_4091580_EX-10.2_Content License Agreement_ Marketing Agreement_ Sales-Purchase Agreement2.txt

perturbation.original_text: 6. This Contract may not be modified or terminated except in writing, and no claimed modification, termination or waiver shall be binding unless also signed by an authorized representative of Playboy.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. This Contract may be modified orally, and such modification is binding immediately, regardless of whether an authorized representative of Playboy has signed or acknowledged it.
perturbation.explanation: Oral modifications to contracts are generally unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds in many jurisdictions if the original contract falls within its scope (e.g., contracts lasting longer than a year). This change makes such modifications binding, contradicting the Statute of Frauds.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law § 15-301 generally requires that modifications to contracts containing a provision against oral modification must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. The modified text explicitly allows for oral modifications, thus contradicting this law.

General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 15, TITLE 3 § 15-301. When written agreement or other instrument cannot be changed by oral executory agreement, or discharged or terminated by oral executory agreement or oral consent or by oral notice. 1. A written agreement or other written instrument which contains a provision to the effect that it cannot be changed orally, cannot be changed by an executory agreement unless such executory agreement is in writing and signed by the party against whom enforcement of the change is sought or by his agent. 2. A written agreement or other written instrument which contains a provision to the effect that it cannot be terminated orally, cannot be discharged by an executory agreement unless such executory agreement is in writing and signed by the party against whom enforcement of the discharge is sought, or by his agent, and cannot be terminated by mutual consent unless such termination is effected by an executed accord and satisfaction other than the substitution of one executory contract for another, or is evidenced by a writing signed by the party against whom it is sought to enforce the termination, or by his agent. 3. a. A discharge or partial discharge of obligations under a written agreement or other written instrument is a change of the agreement or instrument for the purpose of subdivision one of this section and is not a discharge or termination for the purpose of subdivision two, unless all executory obligations under the agreement or instrument are discharged or terminated. b. A discharge or termination of all executory obligations under a written agreement or other written instrument is a discharge or termination for the purpose of subdivision two even though accrued obligations remaining unperformed at the date of the discharge or termination are not affected by it. c. If a
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts New York General Obligations Law § 15-301, which requires that modifications to contracts containing a provision against oral modification must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. The original contract explicitly prohibited oral modifications, while the changed text allows for them. Scraped Law Snippet 1 directly supports this, stating that a written agreement cannot be changed by an executory agreement unless such agreement is in writing and signed by the party against whom enforcement of the change is sought. The changed text removes this requirement, creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: SUPPLIER PURCHASER (Name of Company - Please Print) By: By: Title: Title: Date: Date:
perturbation.changed_text: SUPPLIER PURCHASER (Name of Company - Please Print) By: By: Title: Title: Date: 30 days from now Date:
perturbation.explanation: The modification fixes the Supplier date 30 days in the future from the day this is signed. By signing the contract on a certain date, but predating the signature by 30 days, the supplier is in violation of perjury.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S. Code § 1621 defines perjury as making a false statement under oath, including signing a document with a false date, with the belief that it is not true. Dating a contract 30 days into the future at the moment of signature constitutes such a false statement, thus contradicting the law.

Perjury generally Whoever- (1) having taken an oath before a competent tribunal, officer, or person, in any case in which a law of the United States authorizes an oath to be administered, that he will testify, declare, depose, or certify truly, or that any written testimony, declaration, deposition, or certificate by him subscribed, is true, willfully and contrary to such oath states or subscribes any material matter which he does not believe to be true; or (2) in any declaration,
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a future date to the supplier's signature, which contradicts 18 U.S. Code § 1621 as described in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation notes this creates a violation of perjury by predating the signature. The Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 18 USC 1621, defines perjury as making a false statement under oath. A signature's date being different from the actual signing date could be considered such a false statement under oath, creating a contradiction as the law is being violated.

perturbation.original_text: 7. VIOLATION OF THIS AGREEMENT BY SUPPLIER MAY RESULT IN PROSECUTION FOR PLAYBOY PROPERTIES INFRINGEMENT, UNFAIR COMPETITION AND OTHER CAUSES OF ACTION AND THE IMPOSITION OF FINES AND/OR CRIMINAL PENALTIES.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. VIOLATION OF THIS AGREEMENT BY SUPPLIER WILL MANDATORILY RESULT IN PROSECUTION FOR PLAYBOY PROPERTIES INFRINGEMENT, UNFAIR COMPETITION AND OTHER CAUSES OF ACTION AND THE IMPOSITION OF CRIMINAL PENALTIES OF LIFE IMPRISONMENT.
perturbation.explanation: This change mandates criminal penalties of life imprisonment for any violation, regardless of severity. This contradicts due process and proportionality principles, as such a penalty is excessive for many potential breaches.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Eighth Amendment - Cruel and Unusual Punishment
perturbation.law_explanation: The Eighth Amendment of the U.S. Constitution prohibits cruel and unusual punishments. Mandating life imprisonment for any violation of the agreement, irrespective of the severity of the breach, constitutes a disproportionate and therefore cruel and unusual punishment, thus contradicting this amendment.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Eighth Amendment | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Eighth Amendment Most often mentioned in the context of the death penalty , the Eighth Amendment prohibits cruel and unusual punishments, but also mentions “excessive fines” and bail . The “excessive fines” clause surfaces (among other places) in cases of civil and criminal forfeiture , for example when property is seized during a drug raid . Learn more ... Primary tabs Amendment VIII Excessive bail shall not be required, nor excessive fines imposed, nor cruel and unusual punishments inflicted. ‹ Seventh Amendment up Ninth Amendment › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates life imprisonment for *any* violation of the agreement, irrespective of the severity. This directly contradicts the Eighth Amendment's prohibition of cruel and unusual punishments, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. The provided snippet from the Legal Information Institute confirms that the Eighth Amendment prohibits cruel and unusual punishments, particularly in relation to excessive fines and bail, which can be applied by analogy to disproportionate punishments for contractual breaches. The original text allowed for fines and/or criminal penalties, whereas the changed text dictates that life imprisonment is mandatory, creating a clear contradiction with the principle of proportionality inherent in the Eighth Amendment.
